threats.title: claim() Collects ALL Uniswap Position Fees, Stealing Other Users' Shares and Causing Permanent DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.claim() calls pool.collect(type(uint128).max) which sweeps ALL accumulated fees for the entire shared Uniswap V3 position. User's calculated fee share (amount0/amount1) is sent to the token owner, and the remainder (amountCollected - amount) is sent to FEE_TAKER. Since the remainder includes other users' uncollected fees, those fees are permanently stolen. When subsequent users claim, their calculated fee amounts exceed what collect() returns (because earlier collect swept everything), causing underflow at `amountCollected0-amount0` in Solidity 0.7.6 (no overflow checks), wrapping to ~2^256, and reverting the transfer. All subsequent claims are permanently DoS'd.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting error in how a shared Uniswap V3 position's fees are distributed. Every claim after the first one during a period of fee accumulation will fail, permanently locking user funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.collect(address(this), TICK_LOWER, TICK_UPPER, type(uint128).max, type(uint128).max) collects ALL owed tokens for shared position; then amountCollected0-amount0 sent to feeTaker where amount0 may include fees not backed by collected tokens for later claimers

preconditions: Pool has been successfully launched (_launchSucceeded == true)
Trading has occurred generating fees on the Uniswap V3 position
Multiple users hold NFT positions with vested liquidity to claim

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: collect(type(uint128).max) sweeps entire position's tokensOwed; fee taker transfer: amountCollected0 - amount0 includes other users' fees
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First claimer triggers collection of all accumulated fees; fee taker receives other users' portions; subsequent claims revert due to underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: FEE_TAKER receives 100% of non-claiming users' accumulated trading fees as windfall; all other users permanently lose access to both fees and vested liquidity

economic_questions: What is the total fee accumulation rate for typical ILO pool positions?
How many users are typically affected (DoS'd) per pool?
Can a strategic actor time their claim to maximize fee extraction from the shared position?
validation_requirements: Confirm that second claim reverts due to underflow in fee taker transfer
Calculate total value locked per pool that becomes permanently inaccessible
Verify no alternative claim path exists for DoS'd users

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Zero (any position holder can trigger this inadvertently)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Single claim transaction gas cost (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: FEE_TAKER gains all other users' accumulated fees; DoS'd users lose entire vested position value
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - primarily a protocol-breaking bug that results in permanent fund loss

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None - triggered by normal protocol usage
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant fee accumulation on the Uniswap position

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All NFT position holders except the first claimer
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of remaining vested liquidity + accumulated fees permanently locked
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire ILO pool's post-launch value for all but first claimer

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Shared Uniswap V3 position key = keccak256(ILOPool address, TICK_LOWER, TICK_UPPER)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace second claim: burn returns correct amounts, but collect returns only post-first-claim fees; feeGrowthDelta covers full period; underflow confirmed

threats.title: Whitelist Bypass Allows Non-Whitelisted Users to Buy VULT Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In Whitelist.checkWhitelist(), the condition `if (_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex)` fails to block non-whitelisted addresses. For unwhitelisted addresses, _whitelistIndex[to] defaults to 0. When _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0: first clause is false (not 0), second clause `0 > N` is false. So the revert is never triggered, and ANY address can buy tokens from the Uniswap pool, completely bypassing whitelist restrictions.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic error where the default mapping value (0) for non-whitelisted addresses satisfies the bypass condition. The whitelist is rendered ineffective for its primary purpose of restricting token purchases during the launch phase.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _whitelistIndex[to] defaults to 0 for non-whitelisted; condition `0 > _allowedWhitelistIndex` is false when index > 0; revert not triggered

preconditions: _allowedWhitelistIndex set to any value > 0 (normal operation after adding whitelisted users)
_locked == false (whitelist period active but not fully locked)
Buyer is not blacklisted

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _whitelistIndex[non_whitelisted] == 0; condition: 0 > _allowedWhitelistIndex always false for positive index
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any address bypasses whitelist check and can buy VULT tokens up to _maxAddressCap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Non-whitelisted sybil addresses accumulate VULT during restricted launch phase, capturing early price appreciation

economic_questions: How much VULT can a non-whitelisted attacker accumulate across multiple addresses?
What is the expected price appreciation from launch to open trading?
Does _maxAddressCap still limit individual address purchases?
validation_requirements: Confirm non-whitelisted address passes checkWhitelist when _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0
Test with fresh address (index 0) buying from pool
Verify _maxAddressCap is the only remaining constraint

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for swap transactions per address (~0.01 ETH each)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on VULT price appreciation; unlimited addresses possible
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Equal to whitelisted users' expected returns multiplied by number of sybil addresses

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately when _allowedWhitelistIndex is set > 0 and _locked is false
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduces allocation available for legitimate whitelisted users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate whitelisted users who face diluted allocations
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced token allocation and potentially worse entry price

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Boolean logic trace: (0 == 0) || (0 > N) = false || false = false → no revert

threats.title: Per-User Cap Bypass via NFT Transfer Enables Single Actor to Fill Hard Cap
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy() checks `balanceOf(recipient) == 0` to determine if a new NFT should be minted. The per-user cap is enforced against `_position.raiseAmount` of the current NFT. If a user transfers their NFT to another address, balanceOf drops to 0, and they can buy() again with a fresh position and full maxCapPerUser allocation. Repeated indefinitely until hardCap is reached.
threats.investigation_rationale: The per-user cap is the key fairness mechanism in ILO sales. If a single entity can bypass it, they can monopolize the entire offering, violating the economic guarantee of fair distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf(recipient) == 0 check for minting; cap enforced per-position raiseAmount, not per-address cumulative; ERC721 transferable

preconditions: Whitelisted address (or whitelist bypassed per ECON-H002)
Sale is active (within start-end window)
hardCap > maxCapPerUser (multiple users expected)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: balanceOf(recipient) resets to 0 after NFT transfer; new position starts with raiseAmount=0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: buy(maxCap) → transfer NFT → buy(maxCap) → repeat until hardCap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Monopolize ILO allocation; if token appreciates post-launch, attacker captures all upside

economic_questions: What is the typical hardCap/maxCapPerUser ratio (number of iterations needed)?
Does the whitelist check on recipient block the transferred NFT's new holder from participating?
What is the gas cost of repeated buy+transfer cycles?
validation_requirements: Confirm NFT transfer resets balanceOf for original address
Confirm fresh buy() works after transfer with full cap
Test full cycle: buy → transfer → buy

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for N buy+transfer transactions (~N * 0.005 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire ILO allocation profit if token appreciates

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During sale window, before other buyers fill cap
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Other legitimate participants completely excluded from ILO

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of ILO participation opportunity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire ILO allocation minus attacker's share

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace: buy(max, A) → A.transfer(NFT, B) → buy(max, A) confirms cap bypass

threats.title: Missing Upper Price Bound Validation Allows Broken Liquidity Math in ILO Pools
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.initILOPool() validates `sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96` but does NOT validate `initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96`. If a project admin sets tickUpper such that sqrtRatioUpperX96 <= initialPoolPriceX96, the concentrated liquidity position is entirely in one token. The getLiquidityForAmount0/1 calculations in buy() and _saleAmountNeeded() operate on inverted parameters, producing incorrect liquidity values. Investors receive miscalculated position sizes and launch may deploy wrong token ratios.
threats.investigation_rationale: This missing validation enables the project admin to create pools with fundamentally broken math, potentially leading to investor losses through incorrect position sizing or failed launches that trap funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96 && sqrtRatioLowerX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96, 'RANGE') — missing check that initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96

preconditions: Project admin creates ILO pool with tickUpper where sqrtRatioUpperX96 <= initialPoolPriceX96
RANGE check passes because lower < initial and lower < upper

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getLiquidityForAmount0/1 internally swaps parameters when A>B; _saleAmountNeeded may compute 0 saleAmount or 0 liquidity
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Project admin sets invalid range; investors buy with miscalculated liquidity; launch deploys wrong amounts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Investors overpay for positions that are worth less than expected; project admin can extract excess tokens

economic_questions: What happens to liquidity calculation when SQRT_RATIO_X96 >= SQRT_RATIO_UPPER_X96?
Can the launch succeed with incorrect amounts, or does it always revert?
How much value can investors lose through miscalculated positions?
validation_requirements: Test _saleAmountNeeded with price at/above upper tick
Verify if addLiquidity succeeds or reverts with wrong amounts
Calculate exact investor loss for specific parameter combinations

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Project admin role (no capital beyond ILO setup)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Investors' deposited RAISE_TOKEN could be trapped or misallocated
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Up to 100% of raised funds if launch fails and refund deadline is manipulated

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None for attacker beyond project setup costs
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Contained to the specific ILO pool

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All investors in the misconfigured ILO pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: SQRT_RATIO_X96 vs SQRT_RATIO_UPPER_X96 comparison in edge cases
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Run _saleAmountNeeded with price >= upper tick; check getLiquidityForAmount behavior

threats.title: Uniswap V3 Pool Price Manipulation Griefs Launch Permanently
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.launch() requires the Uniswap V3 pool's current sqrtPriceX96 to exactly match the project's initialPoolPriceX96. Since the pool is publicly created during initProject, anyone can swap on it to move the price away from the initial value. The attacker can repeatedly move the price to block every launch attempt between launchTime and refundDeadline, forcing the project into refund state. This doesn't require observing pending transactions — the attacker simply maintains the pool at a different price.
threats.investigation_rationale: An exact price match is an extremely strict condition. Any market participant can trivially move a newly created pool's price with minimal capital. This creates a permanent griefing vector that blocks launches.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_cachedProject[uniV3PoolAddress].initialPoolPriceX96 == sqrtPriceX96, 'UV3P') — exact price match on publicly accessible pool

preconditions: Project initialized with Uniswap V3 pool
Attacker can provide liquidity and swap on the pool
Pool is accessible between initProject and launch

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Exact equality check: initialPoolPriceX96 == sqrtPriceX96 from slot0()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Swap minimal amount on newly created pool to shift price by 1 tick; launch() reverts with 'UV3P'
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing/extortion: attacker forces project into refund state; may short the project token or demand payment to stop

economic_questions: What is the minimum capital needed to move a newly created pool's price?
What are the swap fees paid by the attacker for sustained price manipulation?
Can the attacker profit by shorting the sale token or through extortion?
validation_requirements: Calculate minimum swap amount to move price by 1 tick on empty/low-liquidity pool
Estimate total cost of maintaining price deviation for full launch window
Determine if attacker can recover swap capital after forcing refund

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal for newly created pool (< 1 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: extortion value or competitive advantage
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high ROI if extortion succeeds; griefing cost minimal
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Sustained from launchTime to refundDeadline (default 7 days)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal — pool starts with no or low liquidity
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Only affects the specific project's pool

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Project admin, all investors in associated ILO pools
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Funds locked until refundDeadline; opportunity cost
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire project's raised capital locked during attack window

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Pool liquidity at launch time; swap costs for price deviation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm 1-tick price move blocks launch; confirm no tolerance in price check

threats.title: buy() Allows Griefing Whitelisted Users by Filling Their Cap Without Consent
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy(raiseAmount, recipient) checks whitelist on `recipient` but transfers RAISE_TOKEN from `msg.sender`. An attacker can call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) to fill the victim's per-user cap allocation, spending the attacker's own tokens. The victim's cap is fully consumed, preventing them from participating on their own terms. The NFT is minted to the victim but with the attacker's chosen amount and timing.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks user autonomy in ILO participation. Combined with ECON-H003 (cap bypass), the attacker could fill legitimate users' caps while bypassing their own, monopolizing the ILO.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buy(raiseAmount, recipient) checks _isWhitelisted(recipient) but transfers from msg.sender; no consent mechanism for recipient

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient RAISE_TOKEN and has approved the ILOPool
Victim is whitelisted
Sale is active

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: msg.sender pays but recipient gets position and cap consumed
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) to exhaust victim's allocation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker loses RAISE_TOKEN but victim is denied self-directed ILO participation; combined with cap bypass, attacker monopolizes sale

economic_questions: What is the attacker's net cost of griefing N whitelisted users?
Can the attacker recover value from positions minted to victims (they can't, as NFT is owned by victim)?
Combined with cap bypass (ECON-H003), can the attacker capture the entire ILO?
validation_requirements: Confirm buy() succeeds with msg.sender != recipient
Confirm victim cannot buy again after cap is filled
Estimate gas and capital costs for griefing all whitelisted users

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: maxCapPerUser * N (number of victims) in RAISE_TOKEN (attacker loses this)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; enables monopoly via ECON-H003 combination
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative standalone; positive when combined with cap bypass

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Large RAISE_TOKEN balance (equal to hardCap in worst case)
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Early in sale window before victims can buy
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Victims receive positions they didn't request

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Individual whitelisted users whose caps are filled
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of ILO participation control; forced into position at attacker's chosen amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm msg.sender != recipient works in buy(); victim cannot re-buy

threats.title: Oracle TWAP Period Fallback to Near-Spot Price Enables Whitelist Cap Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: UniswapV3Oracle.peek() uses `period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod`. For a newly created pool with minimal observation history, longestPeriod could be as low as a few seconds, making the TWAP effectively a spot price. An attacker can manipulate the pool's spot price in a single transaction (flash loan or large swap), causing the oracle to return an artificially low ETH estimate for VULT tokens. This allows buying far more VULT tokens than _maxAddressCap should permit, because the underpriced oracle makes the contribution appear smaller.
threats.investigation_rationale: The oracle is the sole enforcement mechanism for the _maxAddressCap in the whitelist. If it can be manipulated, the per-address cap is effectively bypassed, allowing whales to dominate the token launch.
deployment_assumptions: Pool observation history is short (newly created pool)
threats.config_evidence: UniswapV3Oracle.sol lines 31-33: period falls back to longestPeriod which can be seconds for new pools

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint32 period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod; falls back to potentially 1-second TWAP

preconditions: VULT/WETH Uniswap V3 pool has short observation history (< 30 minutes)
Attacker has capital to manipulate pool price
_locked == false, whitelist period active

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: TWAP with period=1s ≈ spot price; spot price trivially manipulable
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan → swap to deflate VULT price → buy large VULT amount (oracle reports low ETH value) → swap back
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Buy 10x _maxAddressCap worth of VULT while oracle reports contribution under cap

economic_questions: What is the earliest observation age of the VULT/WETH pool when the oracle is set?
Can the manipulation and purchase be atomic (same block)?
What capital is needed to move the TWAP/spot significantly?
validation_requirements: Measure pool observation age at oracle deployment
Calculate flash loan amount needed to move price 10x
Verify cap bypass with manipulated oracle

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Flash loan of 100+ ETH to move pool price significantly (requires validation)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas + flash loan fees + swap fees (~0.5 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Buy many multiples of _maxAddressCap; profit from early token access
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on VULT price appreciation; potentially 100%+ if launch is oversubscribed
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block if atomic; or sustained manipulation over short periods

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Flash loan availability for manipulation capital
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: First seconds/minutes after pool creation and oracle activation
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Temporary price dislocation on VULT/WETH pool

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other whitelisted buyers competing for limited allocation
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Diluted allocation as whale takes disproportionate share

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Pool creation time vs oracle activation; observation cardinality; _maxAddressCap=3 ETH
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute effective TWAP with period=1s and manipulated price

threats.title: Permissionless initProject Allows Admin Hijacking of Token Pair Projects
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.initProject() is permissionless (no access control beyond afterInitialize). It sets msg.sender as the project admin for a Uniswap V3 pool address derived from the token pair. An attacker who knows a project's planned sale/raise token pair can call initProject first, becoming the admin. As admin, they can initialize ILO pools with malicious vesting configs (e.g., assigning 99% of liquidity shares to themselves), control the whitelist, and receive project refunds.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the attacker can observe token pair info (from announcements, token deployments, or configuration transactions), they can preemptively claim admin rights. This doesn't require mempool observation — just public information about planned launches.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control on initProject(); _project.admin = msg.sender; first caller wins for any token pair

preconditions: Attacker knows or guesses the saleToken/raiseToken/fee combination
No project has been initialized for that token pair yet
ILOManager is initialized (afterInitialize modifier passes)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: admin = msg.sender without authorization; uniV3Pool address deterministic from token pair
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call initProject with target token pair before legitimate project; gain admin of all ILO pools for that project
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Create ILO pools with vesting configs giving attacker 99% of project shares

economic_questions: How discoverable are token pair plans before initProject is called?
What value can the hijacked admin extract via malicious vesting configs?
Can the attacker also DoS the legitimate project permanently?
validation_requirements: Confirm initProject has no access control
Confirm require(_project.uniV3PoolAddress == address(0), 'RE') prevents overwriting
Test malicious vesting config deployment after admin hijack

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Only gas cost for initProject call (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 99% of project's liquidity shares if ILO proceeds
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Extremely high if legitimate investors deposit into attacker-controlled pool
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to hijack; persistent control thereafter

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before legitimate project team calls initProject
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Legitimate project permanently locked out of their token pair

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate project admin and all investors
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential total loss if investing into attacker-controlled pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm admin hijack gives control over initILOPool vesting configs

threats.title: Zero-Duration Vest Schedule Causes Permanent Division-by-Zero DoS on Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOVest._validateVestSchedule() does not validate that schedule[i].start < schedule[i].end. If a vesting schedule entry has start == end, the _unlockedLiquidity() calculation executes `FullMath.mulDiv(vest.shares * totalLiquidity, block.timestamp - vest.start, (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS)` with denominator = 0, causing a revert. Since _claimableLiquidity calls _unlockedLiquidity, and claim() calls _claimableLiquidity, all claims for affected positions are permanently DoS'd.
threats.investigation_rationale: A malicious project admin can create ILO pools with zero-duration vest schedules, making it impossible for investors to ever claim their tokens after launch. Combined with the fact that refund is blocked after launch, this permanently locks investor funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _validateVestSchedule checks schedule[i].start >= lastEnd but never checks start < end; denominator (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS becomes 0

preconditions: Project admin creates ILO pool with vesting schedule where start == end
Investors buy into the pool
Pool launches successfully

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS = 0 * BPS = 0; FullMath.mulDiv reverts on zero denominator
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Project admin deploys malicious vest config; after launch, all investor claims revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Investors' funds permanently locked; project admin already received leftover sale tokens via _refundProject

economic_questions: Does _validateVestSchedule actually allow start == end?
Is this reachable only for investor vest (index 0) or project vests too?
Can the DoS be circumvented by any alternative path?
validation_requirements: Deploy ILO pool with start == end in vest schedule
Confirm claim() reverts after launch with division by zero
Confirm no alternative claim or recovery mechanism exists

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Project setup costs + sale token deposit
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for ILO setup and launch
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Project admin extracts leftover sale tokens; investors lose everything

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Contained to specific malicious ILO pool

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All investors in the malicious ILO pool

threats.title: launch() Reentrancy Window via Late _launchSucceeded Flag
threats.hypothesis_description: In ILOPool.launch(), the _launchSucceeded flag is set at the very end of the function, AFTER external calls including _refundProject which transfers SALE_TOKEN to projectAdmin, and addLiquidity which interacts with the Uniswap V3 pool. If SALE_TOKEN implements callbacks (ERC777, hooks), or if the Uniswap pool triggers unexpected callbacks, a reentrant call during launch could observe _launchSucceeded == false. While direct double-launch is likely prevented by fund exhaustion, the state machine invariant is violated, potentially enabling claimRefund to execute mid-launch if refundDeadline conditions are met.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated. _launchSucceeded should be set before any external interaction in launch(). The risk is amplified if the ILOManager owner sets refundDeadline close to launchTime.
deployment_assumptions: SALE_TOKEN has transfer callbacks (ERC777, hooks, etc.)
threats.config_evidence: ILOPool.sol launch() sets _launchSucceeded = true at line ~253, after _refundProject transfer at ~247 and addLiquidity at ~237

proof_of_signal.pattern: _launchSucceeded = true at end of function; _refundProject transfers SALE_TOKEN before flag is set; addLiquidity calls Uniswap pool.mint before flag

preconditions: SALE_TOKEN has transfer callbacks enabling reentrancy
refundDeadline <= launchTime (or already passed)
Soft cap met, launch conditions satisfied

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _launchSucceeded checked in refundable() modifier; still false during launch external calls
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: During _refundProject's SALE_TOKEN transfer, reenter to trigger claimRefund; _launchSucceeded is false, refundDeadline may have passed
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Dual state: both refund RAISE_TOKEN and deploy liquidity; investors drain both paths

economic_questions: Can RAISE_TOKEN refund succeed if it was already sent to Uniswap via addLiquidity?
Is there a realistic token that enables this callback?
What state corruption occurs if _refundTriggered is set mid-launch?
validation_requirements: Deploy with callback-enabled SALE_TOKEN
Set refundDeadline = launchTime
Attempt reentrant claimRefund during launch

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Investor position in the ILO pool
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Potentially double-claim: refund + launched position value
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Up to 100% duplication if both paths yield tokens

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: At launchTime when refundDeadline has passed
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol insolvency for the specific pool

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Partial or total loss depending on extent of double-spend
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire pool's raised and sale token value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: SALE_TOKEN callback capabilities; refundDeadline vs launchTime
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace reentrancy path: launch → _refundProject → callback → claimRefund

threats.title: Owner Can Set Fees Above BPS Causing Underflow DoS on All Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setPlatformFee() and setPerformanceFee() have no upper bound validation. In Solidity 0.7.6, if feeBPS >= 10000 (BPS), _deductFees computes `amount0 - FullMath.mulDiv(amount0, feeBPS, BPS)` which underflows (no overflow checks), producing ~2^256. This massive value causes subsequent token transfers to revert, permanently DoSing all claims for projects initialized with the excessive fee. Fees are snapshot at project creation time.
threats.investigation_rationale: While uint16 caps at 65535, values from 10000-65535 all cause underflow. Any new project initialized after a fee change inherits the broken fee. This is a missing validation that enables permanent fund locking.
threats.config_evidence: ILOManager.sol lines 105-113: setPlatformFee/setPerformanceFee have no bounds check; uint16 max = 65535 > 10000

proof_of_signal.pattern: amount0Left = amount0 - FullMath.mulDiv(amount0, feeBPS, BPS); underflows when feeBPS >= BPS in Solidity 0.7.6

preconditions: ILOManager owner sets PLATFORM_FEE or PERFORMANCE_FEE >= 10000
New project is initialized (snapshots the fee)
Project launches; user attempts to claim

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner sets fee > BPS; all new projects have broken claims
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User funds permanently locked in ILO pools; potential extortion by admin

economic_questions: How many projects would be affected before the fee is corrected?
Can the fee be corrected retroactively for existing projects?
What is the total value at risk across affected pools?
validation_requirements: Confirm setPlatformFee accepts values >= 10000
Confirm _deductFees underflows with such values
Confirm fees are snapshot at project creation and immutable per project

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Admin key compromise or malicious admin
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All funds in affected pools permanently locked

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All investors in projects initialized after fee change
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of invested and vested funds
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All raised capital in affected projects

threats.title: Owner Can Set Refund Deadline to Max Value Permanently Locking Investor Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setRefundDeadlineForProject() has no constraints on the refundDeadline value. Owner can set it to type(uint64).max, permanently blocking the refund path (block.timestamp will never reach it). If launch also fails (e.g., pool price manipulated per ECON-H005), both launch and refund are impossible, and all investor RAISE_TOKEN plus project SALE_TOKEN are permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The refund deadline is the safety net for investors when launches fail. If the owner can eliminate this safety net, investors have no recovery mechanism. This creates a unilateral fund-locking capability.
threats.config_evidence: ILOManager.sol line 129: setRefundDeadlineForProject has no validation on refundDeadline value; only requires onlyOwner

proof_of_signal.pattern: setRefundDeadlineForProject sets _project.refundDeadline to arbitrary uint64; refundable() requires block.timestamp >= refundDeadline

preconditions: ILOManager owner sets refundDeadline to type(uint64).max or far future
Launch fails or is blocked (e.g., price manipulation)
Both exit paths now impossible

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: block.timestamp >= type(uint64).max is unreachable; refund permanently blocked
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner sets max deadline; attacker griefs launch; both paths dead
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Funds permanently locked; potential extortion by compromised owner

economic_questions: Is there any alternative recovery mechanism for investors?
Can the owner reverse the deadline setting?
What is the total value at risk across all active projects?
validation_requirements: Confirm setRefundDeadlineForProject accepts type(uint64).max
Confirm claimRefund becomes permanently uncallable
Verify no emergency withdrawal mechanism exists

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Admin key compromise or malicious admin
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All investor funds in affected projects permanently locked

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After investors have deposited but before launch
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total fund loss for affected project investors

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All investors in affected project's ILO pools
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all totalRaised across project's ILO pools

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm both launch and refund are impossible simultaneously
